author: @oliver90owner
text:
Not read all 280+ messages, so forgive me if it has been mentioned before.

First, kW is power and kWh is energy.  They should not be mixed, or newbies might be misled/befuddled.

Second is battery depth of discharge (DoD).  4 kWh of lead/acid will generally only allow 50% DoD unless battery life is seriously reduced.  Further, automotive type batteries (which includes most modern ‘leisure’ batteries) don’t like ant much discharge at all - an automotive battery is kept fully charged by the alternator in fossil vehicles.  Lithium ion batteries can allow far more energy extraction than Pb - although there are differences, dependent on chemistry employed (think here mobile phone cell life cf modern battery EVs.  A phone cell may last 5 years (unlikely, if fully cycled every day) but most BEV batteries should be good for 15 years of use.

author: @Kanuka-Forest
text: Vertical panels don't reach the same high temperatures as flat panels. The warmer panels get the less efficient they are. This is the same as inverters and charge controllers so don't mount equipment in lofts or warm spaces.

author: @Kanuka-Forest
text: Good to see you making videos again Rob. I am a system designer and installer and have built dozens of systems. In my experience you have to way overspec your panel sizes. Nice to make calculations based on 8 hrs of sunlight. I monitor systems each day, on cloudy days panels can go down to 2% of maximum production.

author: @PaulRose-l4p
text:
Have been following you channel for several years and love what you do but In my experience you will only get 4Kwhr per day from these panels in the summer. It will be closer to 2.0Kwhr (or less) over the year.
If you take a 12 hr day (March or September) the average output (0 in the morning to 480w at midday back to 0 at night) will be 240w. Multiply by 8 hours (average output time and charging losses) and this is where the 2.0Kwhr per day comes from.
A second way of looking at it is that 480W panel is expected to produce about 560 Kwhr per year giving about 1.5 Kwhr per day.
For what you are doing this should be plenty but you will struggle in the winter (November to January).
I have been using a similar size system to run an large irrigation system (with occasional kettle and microwave use) with 5kwhr of battery for the past 12 years. 
As I said love the channel and intend to build the generator with serpentine winding. Keep up the good content.

author: @anubisplays1421

author: @MyProjectBoxChannel
text: We live in a AC world but its quite astonishing how many AC appliances will run directly on a high enough DC voltage directly! It's only old-fashioned transformers that need AC. Most devices have switch mode power supplys, and can run directly on DC. Most LED bulbs I have tested work fine on DC. So with a simple high voltage PV string charging a large series string of lead-acid batteries, you technically don't need a inverter, that wastes some of the power. Many AC motors inside appliances are driven by a internal inverter, so can also use DC directly. Universal motors can run on both AC or DC. I think DC is making a comeback

author: @tacianoferreyra5853
text: Hola Robert. Según la mayoría de los artículos que he leído en Internet, Los inversores del tipo Mppt, son de onda pura y hace que sean más eficientes, por ejemplo, a la hora de utilizar aparatos o herramientas eléctricas. Muy interesante tus videos y espero poder ver el siguiente en este fascinante mundo de la energía solar. Saludos desde Argentina.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Hola, un inversor y un controlador de carga son dos cosas diferentes. Suelen combinarse en una sola unidad.
El inversor convierte CC a CA. El controlador de carga toma la salida del panel solar y la utiliza para cargar una batería. El MPPT se aplica al controlador de carga y la onda sinusoidal pura se aplica al inversor. Ningún inversor es realmente una onda sinusoidal pura. Se aproximan a una onda sinusoidal en pasos. Normalmente, de 3 a 5 pasos.

replies.author: @tacianoferreyra5853
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Es correcto. Yo me equivoqué. Me refería al cargador controlador y no al inversor. Saludos.

author: @brymstoner
text:
love everything about this video. i've been running my solar system for nearly 3 years. i have 1 x 400W and 1 x 420W mono panels in parallel, into a victron 250/70 mppt controller, into 3 x 12v200Ah (7.5kWh LFP) batteries, into a renogy 12v3kW inverter. with a victron 30A battery charger for those bad weather days we love so much in the UK.

this small system powers almost my entire flat. the only things not plugged into it are my fridge and combi microwave. i spent 6 months using watt meters to measure my use over time to understand my habits and where i could improve things while i saved up for various parts. turns out there were lots of improvements i could make. i went from ~60kWh a week to now <20kWh a week. given the current rates, this would otherwise be costing me (so far this month) £28.72 - which ain't half bad, but free is always better!

and that's with 4 computers and a 3d printer on 24/7, plus multiple things regularly charging, and i wfh full time. solar has been a lifeline for me. and i've really enjoyed building my system out and learning from my mistakes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @magnetsplace
text:
Can I make a suggestion Robert based on my years of running a small 320w 4 panel system. Try both methods of wiring up your panels in series and in parallel to see what works best for your install. Due to the distance I had to run the wires from the panels to the mppt and the gauge used, running wired in series at a higher voltage gave less drop than running in parallel.

All the best.

author: @thatcheapguy525
text:
really looking forward to this series Robert.
I built a tiny solar system with 2x 25w panels, 2x PWMs and a battery identical to that on my car about 4 years ago. the primary purpose was to run the LED lights in my garage, my little tyre inflator as necessary and jump the car if ever needed (only once to date). during summer the panels just about keep up with the demand from the lights but winter is another story. I plan to build a much better system at some point to run all kinds of stuff properly so I'm intrigued because this project sounds very close.
one thing I came across more recently was the use of cooling pipes on the back of the panels. this apparently has three key benefits: increases panel efficiency by stopping the panel from exceeding its optimal operating temperature, extends panel life, provides a hot water supply.

author: @teddycunningham7568
text:
Solar panels have become very cheep lately . There are many used panels on the market due to solar farms updating thier equipment to take advantage of government subsidies and tax credits it is common to see 350 watt solar panels going for $50 U.S and I know of some going for $ 15. At that rate they can be used for building fences or covering walls .
Anyone wanting to build a whole house system should look into Industrial Flooded Lead Acid Forklift Batteries   they are massive and cost dramatically less than lithium  are  capable of 100% depth of discharge and have a proven track record of being in service well beyond 20 years.

author: @iExistOne
text:
What is the the Amperage output of ther solar panels?
What is the Amperage output of the charge controller?
What type of batteries are you using?
What is the continuous current the batteries need?
What is the Max current the batteries can accept?
What Battery Management System will you be using?
MPPT was developed by Midnight Solar, they publish their development research.
Shen Zen Basen Tech is making some really good batteries, very strong anodes, and 50 AH to 310AH's.
The 280AH batteries like 90 Amps continuous and have a max rating of 400 Amps.
4 12V 280AH batteries weigh approximately 80Kg. The lower the AH, the lower the weight.
The biggest BMS generally available is 150 Amps

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
What is 'a very strong anode'? I know what an anode is but 'a very strong anode' - I have no idea what you mean by that mate - and shouldn't that also have 'a very strong cathode' to go with it? 
The first 6 questions I answered in the video - true you might need to think about it a bit - but for example the panels are 12V at 120w - what do you think the amp output is? The batteries are 12v vehicle batteries lead acid about 1Kwh each (which I said in the video) so what do you think the Ah will be? and what charge controller would you choose?
So, yes - I do expect you to put a bit of thought into it - and with what you said later in your comment you clearly know a fair bit about the subject - so I am surprised you asked.

author: @bascomnextion5639
text: The important difference that needs to be understood between pulse width modulation controllers  PWM and Maximum Power Point Tracking MPPT is the PWM is just a fast switch turning of and on where the MPPT is like a smart transformer it takes a higher voltage , turns it into a lower one at much higher current so Robert could use all his panels in series which would give 36 to 40 volts and the MPPT would convert this to 12 volts at 3 times the current it also means that as the voltage on the panels drops ( sun setting or clouds) the MPPT can still put charge into the batteries where the PWM would no longer be able to. Also many are marked MPPT but are not you need to do your homework and use google to see reviews looking inside a MPPT you will see a transformer or donut shaped inductor a PWM does not have this. Robert you might explain this to your viewers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I already did a video on the difference between MPPT and PWM mate

author: @dougwardle2175
text: Built my own system for our motorhome and the key thing for us was making sure there was an excess of capacity just in case we have more than 5 consecutive non solar days. You’re absolutely correct, there are a number of different ways to get it to work for you and it really depends on budget and personal choice. For example our panels are mounted horizontally on the motorhome roof because that’s the best compromise for our particular requirements. Having built my own LiFePO4 battery there’s no way would I go back to lead acid batteries but that’s simply based on battery longevity and weight. Like you said, use what you’ve got wherever you can

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: A couple of tips. The best angle to use is the best angle that could be used during winter where you have the shortest days. During summer where your daily hours of sunlight are longer you can get away with a poorer angle. Next put way more batteries than you would need for a single days  charge. This way if it happened to be power left in because you didn't use the batteries  fully for the day you can dump the next day to charge on top of it. Reason for this is simple, you don't always get perfectly sunny days which will give you a full charge. On a bad cloudy day count to no more than 20% of the solar panel. You will be using the batteries capacity then from the previous days charge.

author: @davewardvodafone
text:
Great video, I am now 2years into my solar journey and started very much the same way as you are but I had no battery and spent £850 on a 12V 240 ah lithium iron battery.

My experience when choosing charge controllers MPPT from Victron are a bit pricy but run at oven 90% efficient however PWM is 60-80% I have found them to be closer to 60% rather than 80% and harvesting as much power especially in winter this is very important.

As far as battery’s lead acid need to be deep cycle and you can only take them down to 50% of charge or you damage the battery so the capacity of the battery as far as calculation is concerned needs to be halved I.e. 4Kw you can only use 2Kw. If you have 8Kw you can use 4Kw. 

Whereas lithium iron can be taken down to 20% they are more expensive but are more efficient and take up less space and are lighter.

Hope this helps with your project as you say you need to make decisions and sometimes price as a trade off but I have found that spending a bit more is better over time.

I am building a 48V battery sold as in kit form from China and am upgrading to 2.4 Kw of solar with a Solar MPPT Controller and Inverter in one unit. Should be enough to seriously reduce my power bills and for only about £4000.

Regards
Dave

author: @GabrielPettier
text: Another advantage of vertical panels is that instead of having a big production mid day, you can orient them to have a more flat production output, starting earlier in the morning, and lasting later in the day, not just because they face the sun more, but they also capture more ambiant (scattered) light, which makes it easier to scale production without stressing the grid (otherwise to be able to have enough in morning and evening, you have to build a system that outputs way more than you need at noon).

author: @wilsistermans1118
text: Why not making a solar tracking system? I converted my fixed solar array with 36 panels last summer (New Zealand) to a 27 panel array with sun tracking. These 27 panels now produce 20% more electricity as the old fixed system. Using a simple arduino, two light sensors, one old motor and a bunch of steel pipes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: There are lots of reasons why solar tracking is a pain mate. Panels are actually one of the lowest cost items in a solar system - turns out it is usually cheaper just to add more fixed panels. Tracking systems also require looking after - fine if you love them but if you want instal and leave alone - not so good - but it's like everything if you want to do that - go for it.

author: @rafalklepinski7372
text: Careful with NiFe batteries. Anecdotal data would suggest they last for decades, but that's under specific use cases. Suppliers are very few (only 2) and results are severely lackluster. They seem ill-suited for solar applications based on experiences of people who've tried. Their voltages vary tremendously, they're difficult to fully charge, and with heavy discharge cycles they do not last more than Pb. Perhaps they were made differently half a century ago or they're mostly useful as stand-by power for rare and occasional discharges. I too was tempted by the legend of their longevity but best to steer clear. PWM controllers tend to piss away too much power. A small Victron 100/30 MPPT controller will pay for itself when it extends the life of your battery by a few years thanks to fully charging them daily. England is not known for setting insolation records after all :) I derate panels by a massive 90% to obtain the energy I want on fully overcast days.

author: @HowtoSpeakJapanes
text:
Even though you said (and I agree) that everyone should configure the system in a way he likes or needs, I wonder why you only got so low capacity panels. New panels with 420W are current standard and imho you could never get enough. I run 2 of these facing SSW in combination with a 1.6 kWh Battery. This is stil connected to the grid, so I still get a big share of my energy from the grid.
It is helpful to install higher output (Watt peak) panels as it also helps you in low light times to get a decent output. A 420W panels will give you a higher output when the sky is overcast than 2 small panels of 200W each can do.
The advantage I see in your system is that you minimize transformation losses as you feed and consume directly to/from the battery. 
Regarding the orientation of the panels, 35° is seen as a kind of standard in northern Europe. My panels are now at about 35°, but in late fall I will switch them to 55° and 90° (wall mounted) to get better performance during the low (angle) light period November to April.

FYI, also north of Europe, 53.55° latitude

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: because the point for me is to demonstrate a redundant system architecture. You are quite right I could have done it in one panel - but then I would have missed one of the main points I want to make.

author: @anthonycali6880
text:
I have:
4 - 100W for 400W of Renogy solar panels.
2 - 12V 100AH Lithium-ion batteries for 2400W of storage. 
1 - 4000W Egscatee inverter
   -   -   -
I had three problems.

1. I drew too much current and ruined my 200A circuit breaker. Replaced with same.
2. The inverter, a 2000W Spartan, died so I needed to replace. Learned I needed to mount flat, not vertically. 
3. For long dark periods, I bought a charger without a fan that died, replaced it with a 20A with fan which is twice the current of defective one.
One more problem long wites with extensions, the connectors.  I removed and shorted connectors which gave me problems.

With this setup for past 3 years. I've run these appliances.  
Air conditioner for 4 hours, mainly for testing system.
Toaster, Toaster oven, espresso machine, coffee machine, kitchen TV, kitchen lights under cabinet,  fans, etc.

I am in Massachusetts.  I made two of these setups of
two solar panels on plywood with a stick in back on hinge  to hold them up.

When or if needed, I just walk to my backyard and move the panels. I've learned I can keep them in one place for the mist part.  Snow, just get out and clean them. 

For my purposes,  
400W panels to 2400W is the perfect ratio. 
And in winter, fall, spring, where there's l humidity,  panels can give me like 95% of their rated value.

author: @donaldhoudek2889
text: Robert, Your are missing a lot of power by using a PWM charge controller on your (4) 100 watt (Rated) solar panels. Think about it. You are using 12VDC batteries, so the most your panels will give you (in parallel) is about 271 watts (the rest is clipped), 22Amps @ 12VDC (battery voltage). For MPPT you will get 400 watts, 32 Amps, which is 30% more. Add those 130 extra watts over the average 5 hours of sunlight and that is a pretty good amount of power being lost. You could add another 3d printer to that load. Also, your 12VDC batteries should not be discharged below 12.2 volts or they will deteriorate faster. A dead 12volt battery is 10.5 volts, which you will probably come close to hitting over night. Whereas, the LiFeP4o batteries can be discharged all the way down. I will let you know how vertical mounted panels vs angled panels work out as I am getting ready to install 5kW of bifacial panels vertically in a few weeks. They also will be able to be angled, vertical for hail protection. One last thing, I just looked my the Morning Star MPPT charge controller that I mounted on my RV (250 watt, 34vdc) in 2012 and here are the numbers (68,337.1 AH, 918kWh). The run time shows 11 years, 31 days and 9 hours since I turned it on. Good luck with your project. Great video

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I said all that in the video mate. I also explained why I was choosing PWM - so, it is an informed choice and my choice. I get it you would do something different and I understand why you would do  it. I happen to disagree in my particular circumstances and I don't mind if someone makes a different choice. That's all there is to say really.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It will be interesting to see how this turns out. The one thing I did learn over the years is that you can never have enough solar or money. Per one of the other comments, do not give up on the 3D projects, I love them. Top of the day to you mate.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
aha, a subject i have plenty of experience with, i have 2 x220W solar panels (bought second hand for £40 each) on my shed roof they lay flat (actually they face north at a very slight angle- that is mainly because they are on the north side of my house, so they only get direct sunlight ain the afternoon- a restriction I accepted due to the space i have. my two solar panels, one goes to a PWM charge controller and 4 110Ah leisure batteries (given to me by a friend when they became inefficient on his boat and he replaced them - I figured they would last a bit longer under constant charge conditions- and so far they are about 3 years on and still holding charge) these power a 1kW inverter, which powers 90% of the machines in my shed. the only machine it cant power is a 1919 era southbend 13" lathe. I dont work in my shed every day, so when I run the batteries down, they can charge over a few days.
The second panel runs via a mppt controller to a single 100Ah lithium battery, this battery can be connected to a 300W inverter, and used to charge power tool batteries, or it can be removed to power a small electric outboard motor, it can also be reconfigured to charge a couple of smaller lithium batteries for other purposes.
Now there is a one stop alternative- its more expensive, but you can opt for a solar power generator, a battery bank with built in charge controller and inverter, I recently purchased on on a july 4th special deal and got an 1800W version, with an expansion battery and a 220W bifacial solar panel- this can be taken out and about to provide power when I am away from home and need electricity- eg when on the boat. this as i say is a one stop shop, mine cost over £1000, so probably twice what you would pay if you bought the individual components. but this will run my 13" lathe, it will run the fridge on the boat for most of the day and overnight, during the day it can be charging while providing power. so if you dont want to go to the effort to build your own system, this could be an alternative.
if you are considering solar panels, look into whether you can buy cheap second hand ones, they might not give 100% output any more, but if you can buy more it will make up for that, and they will have decades of life left in them before getting to a point of uselessness.
pwm charge controllers can be got really cheap, under £4 at temu, under £10 on ebay- for 20-30A controllers, MPPT from about £30.
experiments i have done with solar panels, well i know a 30W heater element wont heat a hot tub, but it might stop it cooling while its not heating- I would need a lot more solar panels to generate enough to heat a hot tub, but a hot tub in the shed you are charging batteries will keep the temperature in there above freezing all winter.

author: @jasonstclair1329
text: Always go bigger than you think you’ll need , saves money in the long run. Minimum 60 amp mmpt charge controller, 100ah lithium. 2000w pure sine inverter.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I dunno mate - something about that doesn't gel with me. I can understand what you are saying but I can foresee problems too. Going bigger costs more, it maybe far more than you need and sets a yard stick you might not achieve - how about going for adaptable in which case you will get something done but can expand it if you want to?

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Inverters work best/efficient at about 80/90% of their continuous rated output. So it's sensible to aim at your average use to get about that size inverter. Most inverters will cope with a bit of overload, (some are  rated at 200% of continuous) which is handy for surges/starting motors etc. I did an energy budget (similar to Rob's suggestion), bought just enough panels to start with (2) 🙂, but figured an extra 20-25% of my average for the inverter. I also have a much lower output inverter, for when I'm only using/charging laptop/mobile etc. The lighting is LEDs run direct from the batteries. As of today I'm only now thinking of an extra panel, because I've started to use a bit more on average, and my batteries/panels are only just coping. It was insufficient battery storage that caught me out first.

replies.author: @jasonstclair1329
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering- not going bigger costs more . I started out small as you are now. Went through many variations of lead acid batteries that have a very short lifespan. Lithium in the long run is cheaper as their lifespan is 10 years with much higher performance. I started small with a 1000w inverter and now using a 3500/7000 max pier sign wave. Now I am able to run bigger electronics during power outages. The moral of the story is if you start small and ever plan on expanding, you will have a bunch of small cheap equipment laying around that you will not be able to recoup $ . This is just my experience with upgrading over the last five years.
